body_txt: The most straightforward version of the trope in an action series is that the "fire type" in a Classical Elements Ensemble is also The Hero of the story or The Leader of The Team . However even in narratives that don't directly invoke "the elements", and settings that don't have powers at all, fire symbolism may still follow the protagonist . There are many reasons why fire is associated with heroism. Fire is the element of passion, anger, romance, and other daring traits in the face of a challenge. But around friends, it's no accident that kind souls are described as 'warm'. It's also flashy and visually spectacular, making it a convenient way to draw attention to important characters. Fire is also functionally and symbolically simple. Making fire is a versatile survival skill, useful for keeping warm and cooking, sending up smoke flares , and lighting dark areas . And as you get more refined in harnessing heat energy you get more complex inventions and dangerous weapons. We've been throwing fire ever since we knew how to make it. What's a more heroic image than to smite wickedness with blazing flames ? And what gives more finality to destruction than to see the foe melted down and burned up? Further, The Discovery of Fire represents innovation in mythology: there are various legends of brave and clever humans stealing fire from the gods , kickstarting civilization as we know it. Fire is what makes us pioneers. And every sunrise marks new opportunities. A character using fire has the potential to light the path forward , lead the way , and draw allies in together . Please note that this trope specifically covers the tendency for story leads to be fire-based. It is rare that any story with Elemental Motifs at play or magical Elemental Powers on display lacks a role for fire completely. Evil Is Deathly Cold makes a good corollary to this trope as a thematic contrast between a heroic fire user and a villainous ice user. Contrast Evil Is Burning Hot for when it's the villains who wield the flames. Note though that the two tropes are not mutually exclusive, should you literally be fighting fire with fire. Compare with Light Is Good . For expansions, subversions, and other common ways to play with this trope, see Fire's close relationships at Light/Fire Juxtaposition , Lightning/Fire Juxtaposition , Fire/Water Juxtaposition , and Fire/Ice Duo .
